name: EDRSilencer or Similar EDR Silencing Tool Execution
query: process where host.os.type == "windows" and event.type == "start" and (process.name : ("EDRSilencer.exe", "FireBlock.exe", "EDR-Freeze.exe", "Backstab.exe", "EDRSandBlast.exe") or (process.command_line : "*blockedr*" and process.command_line : "*block*"))
description: Detects execution of EDRSilencer or similar tools designed to disable endpoint detection and response solutions by blocking their outbound network communications via Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) APIs. These tools prevent EDR telemetry from reaching cloud backends, effectively blinding security monitoring.
author: F0RT1KA
false_positives: None expected - these are known offensive security tools
note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating EDR Silencing Tool Execution

EDRSilencer and similar tools use Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) APIs to create network filters blocking outbound traffic from EDR processes. This prevents security telemetry from reaching cloud backends.

#### Possible Investigation Steps
- Verify the process hash against known EDRSilencer samples.
- Check if WFP filters were successfully created (Event ID 5441).
- Examine whether EDR processes lost cloud connectivity after execution.
- Identify the parent process to determine initial access vector.
- Look for concurrent defense evasion or lateral movement activity.

#### False Positive Analysis
- No known false positives for these specific tool names.

### Response and Remediation
- Immediately isolate the affected host.
- Remove any WFP filters created by the tool: `netsh wfp show filters` then identify and remove malicious entries.
- Restart EDR services and verify cloud connectivity.
- Investigate how the attacker gained administrator-level access.
- Deploy application control policies to prevent unauthorized tool execution.

name: WFP Library Load by Process from Suspicious Directory
query: library where host.os.type == "windows" and dll.name : "fwpuclnt.dll" and not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\*", "C:\\Windows\\SysWOW64\\*", "C:\\Program Files\\*", "C:\\Program Files (x86)\\*") and process.executable : ("?:\\Users\\*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*", "?:\\Users\\*\\Downloads\\*", "?:\\Users\\Public\\*", "?:\\ProgramData\\*", "?:\\Windows\\Temp\\*")
description: Detects a process from a user-writable directory loading fwpuclnt.dll, the Windows Filtering Platform user-mode API library. Legitimate processes that interact with WFP typically reside in System32 or Program Files. A process loading this library from a temporary or download directory may indicate an EDR silencing tool or similar WFP manipulation tool.
author: F0RT1KA
false_positives: VPN clients or firewall management tools installed to non-standard locations
note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating WFP Library Load from Suspicious Directory

The Windows Filtering Platform (fwpuclnt.dll) provides APIs for creating network filters. When loaded by a process from a user-writable directory (Temp, Downloads, Public), this strongly suggests a tool designed to manipulate network filtering -- potentially to block EDR communications.

#### Possible Investigation Steps
- Identify the process that loaded fwpuclnt.dll and calculate its hash.
- Check if the process also contains strings referencing EDR process names.
- Look for subsequent WFP filter creation events (Event ID 5441).
- Check for EDR connectivity issues after this event.

#### False Positive Analysis
- Legitimate VPN or firewall tools installed to non-standard paths may trigger this rule. Add known-good process hashes to exceptions.

name: EDR Process Network Connection Blocked via WFP
query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=10m
  [process where host.os.type == "windows" and event.type == "start" and
   process.executable : ("?:\\Users\\*", "?:\\ProgramData\\*", "?:\\Windows\\Temp\\*") and
   not process.executable : ("?:\\Users\\*\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\*")]
  [network where host.os.type == "windows" and event.action == "connection_attempted" and
   event.outcome == "failure" and
   process.name : ("MsMpEng.exe", "MsSense.exe", "SenseCE.exe", "SenseIR.exe",
                   "MpDefenderCoreService.exe", "NisSrv.exe", "CSFalconService.exe",
                   "SentinelAgent.exe", "SentinelServiceHost.exe", "CylanceSvc.exe",
                   "cb.exe", "CarbonBlack.exe", "elastic-agent.exe", "elastic-endpoint.exe")]
description: Detects a sequence where a process starts from a user-writable directory followed by an EDR process failing to make an outbound network connection within 10 minutes. This pattern is characteristic of WFP-based EDR silencing tools that block security product communications after execution.
author: F0RT1KA
false_positives: Network outages or proxy changes causing temporary EDR connectivity failures
Firewall policy changes during maintenance windows
note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating EDR Process Network Blocking Sequence

This sequence rule detects a two-step pattern: (1) a suspicious process starts from a user-writable directory, then (2) an EDR process fails to connect outbound. This strongly indicates WFP filter manipulation to silence EDR telemetry.

#### Possible Investigation Steps
- Identify the process in the first event and verify if it is a known EDR silencing tool.
- Check WFP filter state: `netsh wfp show filters`.
- Verify if EDR cloud connectivity is currently blocked.
- Check for multiple EDR processes experiencing connection failures simultaneously.

### Response and Remediation
- Isolate the host immediately.
- Remove malicious WFP filters.
- Restart EDR services and force a check-in.
- Investigate lateral movement from this host.

name: Process Command Line Targeting Multiple EDR Products
query:
process where host.os.type == "windows" and event.type == "start" and
  process.command_line : ("*MsMpEng*", "*MsSense*", "*SenseCE*") and
  process.command_line : ("*CSFalconService*", "*SentinelAgent*", "*CylanceSvc*", "*CarbonBlack*", "*elastic-agent*") and
  not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\*", "C:\\Program Files\\*", "C:\\Program Files (x86)\\*")
description: Detects process execution with a command line or binary containing references to multiple EDR vendor process names from different vendors simultaneously. Legitimate software rarely references processes from competing security products. This pattern strongly indicates an EDR enumeration or silencing tool.
author: F0RT1KA
false_positives: Security assessment or compatibility checking tools that enumerate installed security products
note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating Multi-Vendor EDR Process Targeting

Tools like EDRSilencer embed a list of process names from many EDR vendors. A single process referencing multiple competing EDR products is highly anomalous.

#### Possible Investigation Steps
- Examine the process binary for embedded EDR process name strings.
- Check if the binary imports WFP APIs (fwpuclnt.dll).
- Verify if any WFP filters were created targeting EDR processes.
- Investigate the process execution chain (parent process, user context).

name: WFP Filter Modification by Non-System Process
query:
any where host.os.type == "windows" and event.code : ("5441", "5444", "5446") and
  not winlog.event_data.ProcessId : ("0", "4") and
  not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\services.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\lsass.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\BFE.DLL")
description: Detects Windows Filtering Platform filter addition, deletion, or modification events (Event IDs 5441, 5444, 5446) initiated by a non-system process. Legitimate WFP filter changes are typically performed by svchost.exe hosting the Base Filtering Engine (BFE) service. A non-system process modifying WFP filters may indicate an EDR silencing tool creating filters to block security product communications.
tags: Data Source: Windows Security Event Log
author: F0RT1KA
false_positives: Third-party firewall products managing WFP filters
Windows updates modifying network filter configuration
note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating WFP Filter Modification

Windows Security Event IDs 5441 (filter added), 5444 (filter deleted), and 5446 (filter modified) track WFP filter changes. When initiated by a non-system process, this may indicate EDR silencing.

#### Possible Investigation Steps
- Identify the process that initiated the WFP filter change.
- Examine the filter details to determine what traffic is being blocked.
- Check if the blocked traffic targets EDR cloud endpoints.
- Verify if the filter was created for legitimate purposes (VPN, firewall).

### Response and Remediation
- Export current WFP filter state: `netsh wfp show filters file=C:\IR\filters.xml`
- Remove suspicious filters blocking EDR communications.
- Restart the Base Filtering Engine service if needed.
- Enable WFP audit logging for ongoing monitoring.

name: Executable with EDR-Targeting Name Created in User-Writable Directory
query:
file where host.os.type == "windows" and event.action == "creation" and
  file.extension : ("exe", "dll") and
  file.path : ("?:\\Users\\*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*", "?:\\Users\\*\\Downloads\\*", "?:\\Users\\Public\\*", "?:\\ProgramData\\*", "?:\\Windows\\Temp\\*") and
  file.name : ("*silencer*", "*edrblock*", "*fireblock*", "*edr-freeze*", "*backstab*", "*edrsandblast*", "*edr_kill*", "*edrkill*")
description: Detects creation of executable files with names matching known EDR silencing or tampering tools in user-writable directories commonly used for staging attack tools. This may indicate an adversary preparing to disable endpoint security controls.
author: F0RT1KA
false_positives: Security researchers downloading tools for analysis
Legitimate EDR management utilities with similar naming
note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating EDR-Targeting Tool Staging

Adversaries commonly stage offensive tools in user-writable directories before execution. File names matching known EDR tampering tools warrant immediate investigation.

#### Possible Investigation Steps
- Calculate the file hash and check against threat intelligence.
- Identify the process that created the file.
- Check if the file was subsequently executed.
- Look for download artifacts (Zone.Identifier ADS) to determine the file source.

name: Multiple EDR Process Connection Failures in Short Window
query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=5m
  [network where host.os.type == "windows" and event.action == "connection_attempted" and
   event.outcome == "failure" and
   process.name : ("MsMpEng.exe", "MsSense.exe", "MpDefenderCoreService.exe", "NisSrv.exe",
                   "SenseCE.exe", "SenseIR.exe")]
  [network where host.os.type == "windows" and event.action == "connection_attempted" and
   event.outcome == "failure" and
   process.name : ("MsMpEng.exe", "MsSense.exe", "MpDefenderCoreService.exe", "NisSrv.exe",
                   "SenseCE.exe", "SenseIR.exe")]
  [network where host.os.type == "windows" and event.action == "connection_attempted" and
   event.outcome == "failure" and
   process.name : ("MsMpEng.exe", "MsSense.exe", "MpDefenderCoreService.exe", "NisSrv.exe",
                   "SenseCE.exe", "SenseIR.exe")]
description: Detects multiple consecutive outbound connection failures from EDR/security processes within a 5-minute window. When WFP filters are used to block EDR communications, security processes will rapidly accumulate failed connection attempts. Three or more failures in quick succession strongly suggests active network filtering targeting security product telemetry.
author: F0RT1KA
false_positives: Network outages causing widespread connectivity failures
Proxy or firewall changes during maintenance
VPN connection drops affecting all processes
note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating EDR Connection Failures

Multiple rapid connection failures from security processes strongly suggest WFP filter-based blocking.

#### Possible Investigation Steps
- Check if all security processes are experiencing failures simultaneously.
- Verify network connectivity for non-security processes.
- If only security processes are failing, check WFP filter state.
- Correlate with process creation events for EDR silencing tools.
